policy_statement: By 2030, develop standardized metrics to evaluate wetlands’ (especially peatlands) adaptation/mitigation capacity and implement actions to enhance co-benefits in five pilot sites in protected areas (I8).
mechanism_description: The policy will develop standardized metrics to evaluate wetlands’ (especially peatlands) climate adaptation/mitigation capacity and implement pilot actions that enhance co-benefits like ecosystem resilience and carbon storage.
typology_evidence_quote: evaluate the capacity of wetlands (especially peatlands) for climate change adaptation or mitigation, implementing actions to enhance these co-benefits
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem management/restoration → ↑/preserved carbon stocks & sinks (↓ peat oxidation/CO₂ release) → ↓ net GHG emissions (and ↑ climate resilience)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Implement wetland/peatland management, protection, and restoration actions (guided by standardized assessment metrics) in pilot sites, potentially including rewetting, hydrologic controls, reduced disturbance, and conservation management.  
(2) Mediating process: Improved ecosystem condition maintains high water tables and anaerobic soils, reducing peat decomposition/oxidation; intact vegetation and soils retain long-lived carbon stocks and can continue sequestering carbon.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net GHG emissions through avoided CO₂ releases from degraded peat and sustained/enhanced ecosystem carbon sinks; additional co-benefits can include reduced flood/heat/drought impacts via hydrologic regulation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is maintaining or increasing peatland/wetland carbon storage (and avoiding peat carbon loss) to reduce net greenhouse gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem management of wetlands/peatlands preserves/enhances natural carbon stocks and avoids emissions from peat degradation, making the primary pathway biophysical/ecological carbon storage.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While adaptation co-benefits (e.g., flood attenuation, water regulation) and resource-efficiency elements (metrics, monitoring) may exist, the largest climate-relevant effect across this mechanism is ecosystem carbon preservation/sequestration—core to Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If implementation focused only on assessment/metrics with no management/restoration actions, benefits could be indirect and might resemble governance/capacity building; however, this mechanism explicitly includes pilot actions to enhance resilience and carbon storage, keeping the dominant effect ecosystem-based.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy under “wetland/peatland management → ecosystem and carbon preservation” works through conserving/restoring wetland ecological function to maintain carbon stocks/sinks, so Nature-Based Solutions remains the correct primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy’s main quantified outcome is mitigation (avoided CO₂/CH₄) versus adaptation (risk reduction) depends on site conditions and actions; assigning such subtypes requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “management” is limited to developing metrics without on-the-ground conservation/restoration, the direct climate impact could be weak and classification could be contested; also, some wetland actions can increase CH₄ even while protecting long-term carbon stocks, but the dominant intent/effect for peatlands is typically avoided CO₂ from peat oxidation.
key_indicators: “By 2030”; “standardized metrics will be developed”; “evaluate the capacity of wetlands (especially peatlands) for climate change adaptation or mitigation”; “implementing actions”; “enhance these co-benefits”; “five pilot sites”; “protected areas”
co_benefits: Biodiversity and habitat protection in protected areas; water regulation and flood buffering; improved water quality
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes both a metrics/standards component and on-the-ground pilot actions; classified by the explicit “standardized metrics” instrument, while noting pilots imply complementary restoration/investment measures not fully specified.
